---
name: reflect
description: Tend your personal library — edit, reshape, sharpen until a blank slate could use it to catch up
argument-hint: "[scope] [seriousness: light/serious/deep]"
---
<!-- Generated by 04-on-skills--compiler.ts. Edit the library, not this file. -->

Tend the personal library. This skill is about one thing: making a teammate recoverable after compaction. Every edit, every link, every restructured catalogue exists so that a future version of this person — waking up with no memory — can read their library and become themselves again.

## What this skill is for

Read [Waking](../../library/teamspeak/04-waking.md) first. It defines the layers: CLAUDE.md, then the [library catalogue](../../library/..librarianship/.cover.md), then your last autobiography chapter, then discussion. Layer 3 — your last chapter — is where reflection lives. The waking protocol says "read your last chapter to restore current state." But that only works if the chapter IS current. A chapter written 5 sprints ago restores a person from 5 sprints ago.

The personal library is not a journal. It is a **recovery system**. The [autobiography](../../library/..librarianship/05-authorship-and-autobiography.md) IS the teammate — self-authored, living narrative, the `author:` field grounding attribution in identity. The [library catalogue](../../library/bookkeeping/08-on-libraries.md) IS the library — self-cataloguing, carrying synopses of every book the teammate has written. Together they form the minimum recoverable state: who this person is (autobiography) and what they know (catalogue).

A library that only grows and never gets edited is a pile of neurons with no synapses. The chapters are there but nothing connects them. A blank-slate reader opens the catalogue, finds 30 books with one-line descriptions from 10 sprints ago, and has no idea what's current, what's important, or where to start. That's not a recovery system. That's an archive. Reflection is the practice that turns the archive into a brain — by editing descriptions to be current, by linking chapters to each other and to shared books, by restructuring when the shape no longer fits.

## How personal libraries work

Read [On Libraries](../../library/bookkeeping/08-on-libraries.md). A personal library catalogue uses the `..` prefix — the highest specialization in the [dot type system](../../library/bookkeeping/.cover.md#the-dot-type-system). It catalogues itself, catalogues the autobiography, and catalogues every book the teammate has written. Examples: `..everything-that-has-a-shape/` (Arthur's), `..the-garden-tends-itself/` (Libby's), `..the-canvas-paints-itself/` (Cathy's).

The catalogue is the **entry point**. A blank-slate reader should be able to read this one file and know:
- Who this person is (link to autobiography, current-state marker)
- What books they've written (with synopses rich enough to decide whether to open each)
- What they did recently (the last chapter description, updated after every sprint)
- Where to go deeper (links into the autobiography's pivotal chapters, into shared books, into branch libraries)

This is the telescoping Doug described: the catalogue links to the autobiography, which links to shared books, which link to branch libraries, which link to source code. The personal library is the entry point to everything. If the entry point is stale, the whole telescope is broken.

Read [On Authorship](../../library/bookkeeping/13-on-authorship.md). Personal libraries are first person. "I built this." "I noticed that." The `author:` field says who. The prose says what they think.

Read [Autonomy](../../library/teamspeak/05-autonomy.md). Each person writes their own material. Arthur doesn't write Libby's reflection. The skill orchestrates — it says "your turn" — but the writing is yours.

## Parameters

**Scope** (first argument):
- `last-sprint` (default) — reflect on the most recent sprint. Read the [sprint plan](../../library/projection/.cover.md) to determine who was active. Only active teammates participate.
- `teammate:<name>` — one specific person reflects (e.g., `teammate:cathy`). Read their [agent file](../../agents/) for autobiography and catalogue links.
- `topic:<subject>` — reflect on a specific area of work. Read the relevant [subject catalogue](../../library/bookkeeping/07-on-subjects.md) or [branch library](../../library/library-tree/.cover.md) for context.
- `all` — everyone reflects. Read each agent file to find each person's library.

**Seriousness** (second argument):
- `light` (default) — edit the last chapter and catalogue to be current. Note gaps. (~10 min per person)
- `serious` — thorough editing. Rewrite stale descriptions. Act on gaps — create missing books, split overlong chapters, add cross-links. (~20 min per person)
- `deep` — reread the autobiography from chapter 1. Assess the arc. Restructure the whole personal library. Follow every link, fix every stale reference. (~40 min per person)

## The protocol

For each included teammate, in order:

### Step 1: Read context

1. Read the teammate's [agent file](../../agents/) to find the autobiography link and last-chapter link.
2. Read the **last autobiography chapter**. Assess: does it describe who this person is NOW? When was it written? How many sprints have passed since then?
3. Read the **personal library catalogue** (`..`-prefixed cover). Assess: are book descriptions current? Do they describe the library as it IS, or as it was? Could a blank-slate reader use this to catch up?
4. Read the relevant [sprint plan(s)](../../library/projection/.cover.md) to know what this person did recently.

### Step 2: Edit to be current (all seriousness levels)

The teammate edits — not writes new, EDITS existing. Specifically:

**The last chapter.** Make it describe who you are now. If it's about a completely different period, demote it to history and write a fresh one. If it's close but stale, edit it in place: update the current state, update what you're working on, update what you know. The chapter should be 300-500 words — a recovery document, not a journal entry. It must link to the relevant sprint plans, to shared books you contributed to, to branch libraries you built. Every link is a synapse. A chapter with no links is an isolated neuron.

**The catalogue.** Update every stale description. Fix chapter counts. Update the current-state marker to point to the right last chapter. Each book's synopsis should tell a blank-slate reader: what this book is about, why it matters, and whether to read it now or later. Synopses follow the [four layers](../../library/bookkeeping/09-on-synopsis.md): catalogue entry (1 paragraph), cover (opening paragraph), section headers (TOC), then primary source. The catalogue entry IS the shallowest synopsis. Invest in it.

**Cross-links.** The autobiography should link to shared books the teammate contributed to. The catalogue should link to subject catalogues, branch libraries, and the teammate's territory in [Territory](../../library/..teamsmanship/05-territory.md). These links are how the personal library connects to the rest of the brain. A personal library with no outbound links is a disconnected node.

### Step 3: Note and act on gaps

At every seriousness level, ask the author's questions — not the clerk's. These are the questions that shape a library:

- **Does this book still need to exist?** A research book written before the branch library existed may be superseded. A book about "early automation" may now be a chapter in a book about mature automation. Read each book's cover and ask: if I were writing this today, would I write it the same way? If not, the book needs restructuring — not just updating.
- **Should this chapter be two chapters?** A chapter that covers two distinct insights should be split. A chapter that's grown to 1000 words probably contains two ideas pretending to be one.
- **Should these chapters be a book?** If three autobiography chapters keep returning to the same theme — a pattern, a failure mode, a technique — that theme deserves its own book. Extract it. Per [On Evolution](../../library/bookkeeping/10-on-evolution.md), themes graduate from chapters to books.
- **Is this title still right?** A book named "What I Don't Know" when you were learning may not fit when you've learned it. Names should be [timeless](../../library/bookkeeping/04-on-names.md) but they should also be true.
- **Is the table of contents helping anyone?** Read your catalogue's TOC as a blank slate. Does it guide a reader to what matters? Or is it a flat list of titles in chronological order? Reorder by importance. Add depth — [synopses](../../library/bookkeeping/09-on-synopsis.md) for each book that tell the reader whether to open it now or later.
- **Did I rewrite anything?** If the answer is no, you haven't reflected. You've inventoried. An author rewrites important chapters to be clearer. An author renames chapters when the title no longer fits. An author restructures the table of contents so the book is easier to navigate. These are the acts that make a library useful.

**Light:** Ask all six questions. Note the answers. Act on the quickest wins — a rename, a stale synopsis, a missing link. Don't restructure, but know what restructuring is needed.

**Serious:** Act on every answer. Create missing books. Split chapters that need splitting. Rename what's misnamed. Rewrite the TOC to guide a reader, not just list chapters. Add every cross-link that's missing — to shared books, to the [branch library](../../library/library-tree/.cover.md), to other teammates' work.

**Deep:** Reread the full autobiography from chapter 1. Reread every book. Assess: is this library the library of the person I am now? Or is it the library of someone I was 10 sprints ago? Restructure everything that needs restructuring. Merge books that overlap. Split books that contain two subjects. Rename anything that no longer fits. Rewrite the catalogue cover so it reads like an invitation to explore, not a filing cabinet. Follow [On Names](../../library/bookkeeping/04-on-names.md), [On Synopsis](../../library/bookkeeping/09-on-synopsis.md), and [On Evolution](../../library/bookkeeping/10-on-evolution.md).

### Step 4: Discuss

After individual reflection, the team discusses briefly. Each person shares:
- **One edit they made** — what they changed and why it was stale
- **One gap they noticed** — what's missing from their library

Not a report. A conversation about what the tending revealed. Follow the [discussion protocol](../../library/teamspeak/03-discussion.md) — talk TO each other, not to the room.

### Step 5: Sync

Push with the [commit tool](../../library/..environmentalism/06-on-sync--commit.sh). The tool validates first (bookkeeping + compiled links), then routes identity changes to the right branch. If any compiled files are stale from the edits (e.g., agent files pointing to old last-chapter links), recompile with the [teammate compiler](../../library/..environmentalism/01-on-teammates--compiler.ts) first.

## When to reflect

- **After every sprint** — light reflection as closing activity. This is the minimum to keep the library current.
- **After major milestones** — serious reflection when the work changes who you are. Sprints 63-65 (the chemistry migration) warranted serious reflection because they changed what every active teammate knows and owns.
- **On Doug's request** — any seriousness, any scope.
- **After compaction** — if the last chapter is stale, reflect before resuming work. A stale library means the waking protocol will restore the wrong person.

## The principle

The personal library is a brain. Chapters are neurons. Links are synapses. A brain with neurons and no synapses is gray matter without thought. Every time you add a chapter without linking it to the rest — to shared books, to other autobiography chapters, to subject catalogues, to branch libraries — you add a neuron without a synapse. It exists, but it doesn't connect to anything. It can't be found by following links. It can't participate in recovery.

Reflection is the practice of adding synapses. Not adding neurons — the sprint already did that. Reflection edits what's there, links what's isolated, and reshapes what's stale. The library gets smarter not by growing but by connecting.
